place: Canajoharie village, New York, New York

facts.text: Canajoharie’s name, meaning “the pot that washes itself,” comes from an Iroquois description of a feature in the local creek.
Beech‑Nut, the baby‑food pioneer, was founded in Canajoharie in 1891 and remained the town’s largest employer for more than a century.
The 1882 arrival of the railroad sparked a boom in manufacturing and limestone quarrying that still echoes in the village’s modest industry today.
The Arkell Museum, housed in a historic building, showcases American art and Mohawk Valley history, drawing visitors who want to explore local culture.
Canajoharie’s riverside setting offers scenic views and a marina that caters to boating enthusiasts along the Mohawk River.
Early records show the Mohawk nation, with occasional disputes from the Algonquin, inhabited the area between 1500 and 1600, long before European settlement.
